title: EPIC: Stingray Autonomous Code Health Auditor
description: Periodic Temporal workflow (every 12h) that examines the codebase itself — god objects, tech debt, stale docs, dependency rot, coverage gaps, coupling, OSS bolt-on opportunities. High severity auto-files as P2 beads + notification. Medium/low stored for planning triage. Skips if no commits since last run. Full design: docs/architecture/STINGRAY_DESIGN.md

title: Stingray: Schema and Store Methods

design: Follow schema provided in STINGRAY_DESIGN.md. Make sure to use standard CHUM sqlite patterns.

title: Register custom Temporal search attributes for visibility
description:

- `priority` (Int) — task priority (0-4)
- `agent` (Keyword) — assigned agent name (claude/codex/gemini)
- `current_stage` (Keyword) — current workflow stage (plan/gate/execute/review/dod)
- `task_title` (Text) — task title for search

Implementation:
1. Register search attributes with Temporal server on startup (idempotent)
2. Set attributes via workflow.UpsertSearchAttributes() at workflow start
3. Update `current_stage` attribute on each stage transition
4. Update dispatcher scan to use search attributes for running-count queries

This enables surgical batch operations (e.g., terminate all failed gemini workflows) and makes the Temporal UI much more informative with filterable columns.

- ChumAgentWorkflow sets project/priority/agent/task_title at start
- current_stage updates on each stage transition
- Search attributes visible and filterable in Temporal UI at :8080
- Batch queries using custom attributes work (e.g., agent = "gemini")
- go build ./... succeeds
- go test ./... passes

title: Document biological architecture: atoms, enzymes, phylogeny, immune system
description:
Capture the full biological metaphor hierarchy that has emerged:

## Hierarchy (playbook side)
- **Atoms** = individual tool calls / scripts (smallest indivisible unit)
- **Molecules** = composed sequences of atoms (a single step)
- **Proteins** = composed sequences of molecules (a complete workflow)
- **Enzymes** = calcified scripts — deterministic replacements for LLM calls (already implemented as CalcificationWorkflow)
- **DNA/Genome** = accumulated species memory, antibodies, fossils

## Immune System (defense layers)
- **IgM** (first response) = immediate retry with error context
- **IgG** (long-term memory) = genome antibodies, persists across tasks
- **IgA** (mucosal barrier) = preflight checks, DoD gates, UBS scans
- **IgE** (allergic response) = species hibernation after 3+ failures
- **IgD** (activation signal) = escalation to Hex/human

## Per-Organism Genomes
Each actor has a specialized genome:
- Turtle: planning anti-patterns, sizing, priority frameworks
- Crab: decomposition patterns, dependency graphs
- Shark: execution patterns, file-level antibodies
- Octopus: learning patterns, retro templates

## Phylogeny
Track species evolution over time using git history:
- Most-mutated files = most hostile ecological niches
- git log --stat shows which organisms evolve which files
- Lineage tracking for protein/antibody changes

## Key Separation of Concerns
- Aquarium metaphor = the ACTORS (who does what)
- Molecular biology = the PLAYBOOK (how they do it)
- These are complementary, not competing
acceptance: IMMUNOLOGY.md or equivalent doc exists capturing the full biological metaphor hierarchy, immune system layers, and per-organism genome specializations

title: Context boundaries between plan/execute/review stages
description: Inspired by HankWeave controlled forgetting: each pipeline stage should get only the context it needs, not the entire accumulated history. Currently the execute step gets plan.PreviousErrors growing without bound across retries. The review step gets the full execution output. Implement explicit context windows: plan stage gets task prompt + lessons. Execute stage gets plan summary + previous errors (trimmed to last 2). Review stage gets plan + diff only. DoD gets only the check commands.

For CodeReviewActivity, pass only plan.Summary + truncated diff instead of full execResult.Output. Add context size logging at each stage boundary: logger.Info("context boundary", "stage", name, "promptLen", len(prompt)).

title: Sub-step execution isolation (codon-inspired granularity)
description: Currently ExecuteActivity is a single monolithic LLM call. When it fails, we retry the entire execution. Inspired by HankWeave codons: break execution into sub-steps with git checkpoints between them. After plan generates FilesToModify and Steps, execute each step as a separate LLM call with a git commit between. If step 4/5 fails, roll back to checkpoint after step 3 and retry only step 4. This cuts token waste on retries by ~60% for multi-step tasks.
acceptance: 1. Multi-step plans execute each PlanStep as a separate agent call. 2. Git commit after each successful step (chum checkpoint). 3. On step failure, git reset to last checkpoint and retry that step only. 4. Single-step plans behave identically to current behavior. 5. Step-level timing logged in StepMetrics.
design: Add SubStepExecuteActivity that takes a single PlanStep + TaskRequest. In ChumAgentWorkflow, if plan.Steps > 1, loop through steps calling SubStepExecuteActivity for each. After each, run git add -A && git commit --allow-empty -m "chum: checkpoint step N". On failure, git reset --hard HEAD~1 and retry. Fallback to monolithic ExecuteActivity if sub-step mode fails twice.

title: Alien Observer: kill stagnating evolutionary branches at sub-step level
description: Last resort above the escalation chain. When sub-step execution retries the same step and gets the same error signal twice, the alien intervenes: kills the branch, rolls back to the last checkpoint, and forces a model switch (different gene pool). The alien does not retry — it exterminates the dead-end and hands the sub-step to a different species. This builds on sub-step checkpointing (chum-f8d7f7): the checkpoint tree creates the branches, the alien prunes them. Every pruned branch feeds the genome as an antibody so future organisms never explore that dead end again.
acceptance: 1. After a sub-step fails twice with similar error (string similarity > 0.7 or identical error category), trigger alien intervention. 2. Alien logs: alien_intervention health event with branch details. 3. Alien forces tier escalation at the sub-step level (not whole workflow). 4. The pruned branch pattern is recorded in the genome as an antibody. 5. If the alien kills 3 branches on the same morsel, the morsel is marked hibernated — the biome cannot support this organism.
design: In the sub-step retry loop (from chum-f8d7f7), after each failure compare current error with previous error using simple string similarity (shared words / total words). If similarity > 0.7: alien intervention. Log health_event(alien_intervention, details). Call EvolveGenomeActivity with antibody for the sub-step pattern. Force escalation to next tier for this sub-step only (steps 1-3 stay on original agent). If 3 alien interventions on same morsel: HibernateGenomeActivity + mark task escalated.

title: Dispatcher: prevent re-dispatching tasks with in-flight workflows
description:
POST-MORTEM FINDING from chum-f8d7f7: The dispatcher dispatched the same task 315 times in 10 hours because it did not check whether a Temporal workflow was already running for that task ID. Each dispatch spawned a CambrianExplosionWorkflow, which spawned multiple ChumAgentWorkflows — resulting in 629 plan generation attempts.

Fix: Before dispatching, query Temporal visibility for running workflows matching the task ID. If any exist, skip the task.

SELF-DETECTION: The system could have caught this with: (a) a health metric tracking dispatch-count-per-task — any task dispatched >3 times in an hour is anomalous. (b) A rate limiter in the dispatcher that caps dispatches per task ID per time window. (c) The strategic groom could monitor for runaway dispatch patterns.

title: Route complex architectural tasks to planning ceremony instead of autonomous dispatch
description:
POST-MORTEM FINDING from chum-f8d7f7: The task "Sub-step execution isolation" asked the shark to restructure the core execution engine — a deep architectural change requiring understanding of workflow.go, Temporal semantics, signal channels, etc. The shark failed at planning 629 times because it lacked the context and architectural understanding.

Fix: Add a complexity classifier in the dispatcher or crab. Tasks that modify core workflow files (workflow.go, activities.go, worker.go) or have labels like execution-engine should be auto-routed to the planning ceremony (turtle) instead of autonomous shark dispatch.

SELF-DETECTION: The system could have caught this with: (a) A planning-failure-rate threshold — if >80% of plan attempts fail, flag the task as too complex for sharks. (b) The octopus learner should detect the pattern "codex/gemini cant even plan this" and recommend reclassification. (c) File-based risk scoring — tasks touching core workflow files get auto-elevated.

title: Enrich StructuredPlanActivity with more codebase context for self-modifying tasks
description:
POST-MORTEM FINDING from chum-f8d7f7: The StructuredPlanActivity prompt likely doesnt include enough of the codebase for the agent to understand the execution engine architecture. When CHUM modifies itself, it needs full context of workflow.go, activities.go, types.go at minimum.

Fix: Detect self-modifying tasks (project=chum) and inject key file contents into the planning prompt. Use the repo map from StrategicGroomWorkflow or a targeted file read.

SELF-DETECTION: The system could have caught this with: (a) Tracking plan-failure reasons — if the agent says "I dont understand the codebase structure", inject more context on retry. (b) The genome could store which files are needed to plan tasks in each species.
notes: [remora] After chum-e1b4bb completed: Recent task chum-e1b4bb added uniform infrastructure_dead failure_category handling after runAgentWithFailover across temporal activities; when implementing chum-plan-context, ensure injected self-modification context includes the updated internal/temporal/activities.go (and matching activity patterns) so planning reflects current dispatch/failure semantics.

title: Add self-monitoring for runaway dispatch and failure loops
description:
POST-MORTEM META-FINDING: The system should detect its own pathological patterns. chum-f8d7f7 burned through 315 Cambrian Explosions and 629 plan attempts without any circuit breaker triggering. The species eventually hibernated but only after the last dispatch cycle — not after the first 50 failures.

Implement: (a) Per-task dispatch rate limiter — max 3 dispatches per task per hour. (b) Health metric: if any task has >10 consecutive failures, auto-pause it and notify. (c) Strategic groom should scan for anomalous dispatch/failure ratios during the daily sweep. (d) Dispatcher should check task failure count before dispatching — dont resend tasks that have failed N+ times without human review.

This is essentially an immune system upgrade — IgM (quick retry) exists but there is no IgA (barrier preventing re-entry of known-bad tasks).

title: All code landing on main must pass DoD — not just shark changes
description:
POST-MORTEM ROOT CAUSE: The systemic blocker (api_test.go missing DAG arg) was introduced by a human-paired agent (Antigravity) working OUTSIDE the CHUM pipeline. The agent ran go build but not go test, and the broken test was invisible to go build because _test.go files are not compiled during go build.

The CHUM sharks then inherited a broken main branch and had zero chance of passing DoD — 10+ hours of wasted compute across 629 plan attempts.

Prevention layers:
1. [DONE] make build now compiles tests (go test -run=^$ ./...)
2. [TODO] Pre-push git hook that runs go test -run=^$ ./... before allowing push to main
3. [TODO] CI workflow on main that runs full DoD checks — any push that breaks tests gets flagged immediately
4. [TODO] Consider running DoD checks on the BASE branch before dispatching sharks — if main is already broken, dont dispatch

SELF-DETECTION: The system should run a lightweight baseline health check (go build + go test compile) on main before ANY dispatch tick. If main itself is broken, pause dispatching and alert — dont send sharks to hunt in poisoned waters.

title: Implement tidal dispatch cycles: build → harden → defragment
description:
Biological analogy: the brain uses sleep to consolidate memories, defragment, and repair. CHUM should have the same rhythm — tidal cycles that alternate between feature work (high tide) and hardening/cleanup (low tide).

Concept: Instead of dispatching feature morsels continuously, the dispatcher should operate in phases:

HIGH TIDE (build phase):
- Dispatch feature/enhancement/bug morsels normally
- Track accumulated tech debt signals (test coverage drops, lint warnings, test flakiness)
- Monitor code churn rate and file mutation frequency

LOW TIDE (harden phase):
- Triggered automatically after N feature morsels land, or on a time schedule
- Pause feature dispatch
- Run baseline health checks on main (go test, go vet, lint)
- Dispatch tech debt/cleanup morsels from the backlog
- Run the strategic groomer to reassess priorities
- Defragment: clean up stale branches, worktrees, dead workflows
- Run the paleontologist to analyze evolution patterns

Implementation ideas:
1. Dispatcher config: tide_cycle_length (e.g. 5 features then 1 hardening cycle)
2. StrategicGroomWorkflow generates hardening morsels automatically
3. Baseline health gate: if main is broken, force low tide immediately
4. The janitor, paleontologist, and strategic groomer all run during low tide

This maps to the circadian rhythm in biology — the system needs rest periods to maintain long-term health.

title: Sanitize crab input: escape FTS5 special chars and reject malformed plans early
description:
The crab ClarifyGapsActivity passes raw scope item text into FTS5 lesson queries without escaping. Words like trigger, state, and characters like / and , cause SQL errors. Also, ParsePlanActivity silently fails on plans without - [ ] checklist items but gives no helpful error message.

Fix: (a) Escape FTS5 special chars in lesson search queries. (b) Add a preflight validation step that checks plan_markdown format before starting the workflow and returns clear error. (c) Consider a normalization step that converts prose plans into checklist format via LLM before parsing.

title: Fix .zshrc sourcing in dispatch env — 52 instant-death failures
description: dispatch_output analysis shows 52 dispatches die instantly (<1KB output) because the dispatch shell scripts source .zshrc which tries to load .acfs/zsh/acfs.zshrc (not found). The agent process never even starts. Fix: dispatch scripts should use env -i sh or skip interactive shell init entirely. This is the #1 infrastructure failure by volume.
acceptance: Dispatch scripts do not source .zshrc or any interactive shell init. Zero instant-death failures (<1KB output) in next 50 dispatches.
design: Modify the shell command construction in the dispatch package to use env -i sh -c or bash --norc --noprofile instead of sourcing the user shell profile.

title: Fix gateway connect failures — 59 crashes + 52 CLI flag injection bugs
description: dispatch_output analysis: 59 dispatches fail with "gateway connect failed: Error: gateway closed (1000)" and 28 fail with "error: required option -m, --message not specified" plus 24 with "Gateway agent failed; falling back to embedded". Root cause: OpenClaw gateway instability + prompt not reaching the codex CLI --message flag (quoting/pipe issue in dispatch script).
acceptance: Zero gateway connect failures and zero missing -m flag errors in next 100 dispatches. Gateway health check added to pre-flight.
design: 1. Add gateway health probe before dispatch (curl gateway endpoint). 2. Fix shell quoting for --message flag — ensure prompt text is properly escaped. 3. Consider adding gateway restart logic or fallback.

title: Fix golangci-lint in dispatch PATH — exit 127 blocks every DoD check
description: DoD results show golangci-lint run --timeout=5m exits with code 127 (command not found) in the dispatch environment. This blocks every cortex/chum morsel from passing DoD. The binary exists on the host but is not on PATH in the dispatch shell context.
acceptance: golangci-lint is available in the dispatch environment PATH. DoD checks for go projects no longer fail with exit 127.
design: Ensure the dispatch environment includes $GOPATH/bin and $HOME/go/bin in PATH. Alternatively install golangci-lint to /usr/local/bin. Check the Docker image or dispatch script env construction.

title: Re-evaluate provider routing — active providers dramatically underperform
description: Data analysis of 1819 dispatches shows codex-spark has 3.5% success on cortex and gemini-pro has 0% on chum — and these are the ONLY enabled providers. Historical providers performed far better: llama-4-scout (62% cortex, 99.9% hg-website), gpt-5.3-codex-spark (73.5% cortex), claude-sonnet-4 (100% on 20 dispatches). The Feb 20 provider switch correlates with a collapse from 82-91% to 0-35% success.
acceptance: Provider tier routing in chum.toml updated with providers that have >50% historical success rate on cortex/chum. Completion rate recovers to >60% within 24h.
design: Update [tiers] section in chum.toml. Consider re-enabling llama-4-scout or gpt-5.3-codex-spark for fast/balanced tiers. Keep gemini-pro for premium only if needed.

title: Add output-size early-kill for infrastructure-dead dispatches
description: Analysis shows failed dispatches average 2.6KB output vs 39.9KB for successes. 52 of 220 failures have <1KB output — the agent never starts reasoning. These waste timeout duration (up to 45min for balanced tier) waiting for an already-dead process. Add an early-kill mechanism: if agent output is <1KB after 60 seconds, the dispatch has infrastructure-failed. Kill immediately and reclassify as infrastructure_failure.
acceptance: Dispatches with <1KB output after 60s are killed and classified as infrastructure_failure. Average wasted time for dead dispatches drops from timeout duration to <2 minutes.
design: Add output size monitoring in the dispatch loop. After initial startup period (60s), check output_bytes. If below threshold (1024 bytes), send SIGTERM, mark as infrastructure_failure, and skip retry escalation.

title: Fix OpenClaw tool-call validation — read-without-path causes OOM kills
description: dispatch_output shows agents getting stuck in a tight tool-call loop: [agent/embedded] read tool called without path (7+ times observed) followed by OOM kill (status 137). The agent calls read with no path argument, gets EISDIR error, and retries infinitely until killed. This is a tool-call validation gap in OpenClaw — args should be validated server-side before execution.
acceptance: OpenClaw read tool rejects calls with empty/missing path argument and returns an error to the LLM instead of executing. No more OOM kills from tool-call loops.

title: Hibernate black-hole morsels — 74 wasted dispatch slots with zero results
description: Three morsels have consumed 74 dispatch slots with 100% escalation and zero completions: cortex-f51j (38 dispatches), cortex-j9j2 (18), cortex-o4ni (18). These are stuck in an infinite escalation loop. They should be hibernated or manually triaged to stop wasting resources.
acceptance: Morsels cortex-f51j, cortex-j9j2, and cortex-o4ni are marked as hibernated or blocked. They no longer appear in dispatcher candidate scans.

title: Debug calcifier — 11+ hg-website morsels exceed threshold but no scripts produced
description: Data shows 11+ hg-website morsels at 100% success rate with 15-100+ dispatches each (e.g. bd-1ag=102, bd-3le=97, bd-pjs=97). The calcifier compile_threshold is set to 10, yet the calcified_scripts table is empty and only 3 proteins exist with zero folds. DetectCalcificationCandidatesActivity is either not finding these candidates or the calcifier workflow is not running. Debug and fix.
acceptance: DetectCalcificationCandidatesActivity correctly identifies morsels with consecutive successes >= compile_threshold. At least 5 hg-website morsels produce shadow calcified scripts.
design: Check: 1. Is the calcifier schedule registered? 2. Does GetConsecutiveSuccessfulDispatches work with morsel_id prefix matching? 3. Is the hg-website project missing from calcifier config? 4. Are the old bd- prefixed morsel IDs causing type-detection issues?

title: Separate completed from DoD-passed as the primary success metric
description: Current dispatch completion rate is 73.3% but DoD pass rate is only 24.0% (55/229). The system reports completion as success but most completions fail quality checks. Add a DoD-verified success metric that tracks only dispatches that both completed AND passed DoD. Surface this in paleontologist reports, Matrix alerts, and morning briefings.
acceptance: A new dod_success_rate metric is tracked alongside completion_rate. Paleontologist reports and failure rate trending use DoD-verified success as the primary quality signal.

title: Inject success behavioral patterns into genome antibodies
description: dispatch_output analysis reveals clear behavioral fingerprints that predict success: agents that ran go test (25% of successes, 0% of failures = perfect predictor), agents with explicit planning (5% but 0% failure), agents that checked DoD criteria (58% success vs 5% failure), agents that read before writing (33% vs 25%). These patterns should be injected into genome antibodies so all agents are nudged toward these winning behaviors via CLAUDE.md or prompt preamble.
acceptance: Genome antibodies or CLAUDE.md rules enforce: 1. Run go test before marking complete. 2. Read relevant files before editing. 3. Check acceptance criteria early. Success rate for new dispatches improves measurably within 48h.
design: Add antibody entries to the genome system via SynthesizeCLAUDEmdActivity or seed manually. Key rules: always-run-tests, read-before-write, plan-before-code, check-dod-early.

title: Bootstrap Compound-style knowledge, memory, and genetics in CHUM
description:
# Compound Knowledge/Memory/Genetics Program

Implement a CHUM-native compounding stack that turns prior work into reusable memory,
operational product loops, and deterministic execution genes.
acceptance:
- CHUM can ingest compounding artifacts (plans, reviews, fixes, learnings) from local repositories into durable memory records.
- Planning and review workflows can retrieve relevant memory hits with bounded prompt budgets and source citations.
- Product feedback reports can be transformed into PRDs and executable DAG tasks with deterministic progress tracking.
- Successful multi-step execution traces can be crystallized into reusable deterministic molecules/proteins with promotion gates.
- Each subsystem ships with independent feature flags, metrics, and rollback controls.
design:
This epic maps three external patterns into CHUM-native primitives:
1. Every Compound Engineering workflow -> memory corpus + retrieval-augmented planning/review.
2. Compound Product workflow -> report analysis -> PRD -> one-task-per-iteration execution loop.
3. Dex skill crystallization -> successful trace extraction -> deterministic reusable flow promotion.

Decomposition:
- chum-td26a: ingest/index compounding artifacts as queryable memory.
- chum-td26b: inject retrieved memory into plan/review prompts.
- chum-td26c: implement report-to-PRD task loop ingestion.
- chum-td26d: capture execution traces and generate crystallization candidates.
- chum-td26e: promote validated crystal candidates into proteins/calcified scripts.
- chum-td26f: rollout sequencing, observability, and operational guardrails.

title: Ingest Compound artifacts into CHUM memory corpus
description:
First slice: build the memory substrate so CHUM can query prior high-signal
engineering and product artifacts instead of re-solving from scratch.
acceptance:
- Add a durable `compound_artifacts` store that captures source, artifact type, normalized content, tags, and content hash.
- Support ingestion from configured local source roots for:
  - `EveryInc/compound-engineering-plugin` (`docs/solutions`, `.claude/commands`, `.claude/skills`)
  - `snarktank/compound-product` prompts/templates/workflow docs
  - optional local drop-in directories
- Maintain both FTS and embedding indexes for retrieval; embeddings are generated asynchronously.
- Re-ingestion is incremental and idempotent using content hash + source path dedupe.
- Expose ingestion counts (inserted/updated/skipped/errored) in activity results and logs.
- Add unit tests for parser normalization, dedupe behavior, and incremental sync.
design:
**Schema**
- Add table: `compound_artifacts`
  - `id`, `source_repo`, `source_path`, `artifact_type`, `title`, `content`, `tags_json`, `content_sha256`, `ingested_at`, `updated_at`
- Add FTS table: `compound_artifacts_fts`
- Add optional embedding table keyed by `artifact_id` (or reuse existing embedding infra if available).

**Config**
- Extend `chum.toml` with:
  - `[learner.compound]`
  - `enabled`, `source_roots`, `max_file_bytes`, `reindex_interval`

**Temporal Integration**
- Add `SyncCompoundArtifactsActivity` under learner/strategic groom path.
- Activity walks configured roots, parses markdown/text artifacts, normalizes headings into title/sections, and stores records.

**Normalization Rules**
- Classify artifact types: `plan`, `review`, `learning`, `workflow`, `prompt`, `command`.
- Drop boilerplate sections, preserve actionable instructions, and tag with origin metadata.

title: Capture execution traces and extract skill crystallization candidates
description:
Create the raw substrate for Dex-style crystallization: observe what worked,
compress it into reusable deterministic flow candidates, and ignore dead branches.
acceptance:
- Persist normalized per-task execution traces across plan, execute, review, and DoD stages.
- Each trace event records step type, tool/command, inputs, outputs summary, duration, and success/failure.
- Build a crystallizer that filters failed branches and exports successful deterministic paths as candidate flows.
- Candidate flows are parameterized to remove task-specific literals and environment noise.
- Sensitive values are redacted before trace persistence and before candidate export.
- Add tests for trace capture completeness, branch filtering correctness, and parameterization stability.
design:
**Schema**
- `execution_traces` (trace_id, task_id, species, status, started_at, completed_at)
- `trace_events` (trace_id, seq, step, tool, command, outcome, payload_json, duration_ms)
- `crystal_candidates` (candidate_id, species, template_json, support_count, success_rate, status)

**Instrumentation**
- Instrument workflow/activity boundaries and execution adapters to emit structured events.
- Normalize tool invocations from different providers into one canonical event shape.

**Crystallization Extraction**
- Group traces by species + goal signature.
- Remove branches ending in rejection/failure loops.
- Compute longest common successful subsequence and export as a reusable candidate template.

**Output**
- Candidate includes preconditions, ordered steps, verification checks, and required inputs.

title: Inject compound memory retrieval into planning and review prompts
description:
Wire memory retrieval into CHUM's main reasoning surfaces so prior successful
patterns shape new plans and reviews by default.
acceptance:
- `StructuredPlanActivity` retrieves top-k relevant compound artifacts using task context (title, description, labels, recent failures).
- Review stage retrieves context using task context + diff summary + DoD failures.
- Prompt injection uses a bounded "Memory Hits" section with source citations and hard token budget cap.
- Retrieval combines semantic similarity, lexical relevance, and historical success weighting.
- If retrieval fails or corpus is empty, planning/review continues without hard failure.
- Add tests proving deterministic prompt assembly, token capping, and graceful fallback behavior.
design:
**Activities**
- Add `RetrieveCompoundContextActivity(query)` returning ranked context packs:
  - `artifact_id`, `title`, `source_path`, `relevance_score`, `snippet`, `tags`

**Ranking**
- Score = `embedding_similarity * w1 + bm25 * w2 + success_prior * w3 - staleness_penalty`
- Keep top 3-5 hits; summarize each into concise constraints and proven approaches.

**Workflow Hooks**
- Planning hook: before plan generation prompt.
- Review hook: before reviewer prompt.
- Record retrieval telemetry per task (`hits`, `top_score`, `used_sources`).

**Safety**
- Exclude low-confidence or conflicting artifacts unless explicitly requested.
- Enforce strict max token allocation to memory section so core task context remains dominant.

title: Implement Compound Product report-to-PRD-to-task execution loop
description:
Bring compound-product's operational loop into CHUM so product reports become
structured, executable, and continuously tracked work instead of static docs.
acceptance:
- Add a workflow that ingests a report artifact and produces structured findings with evidence and priority.
- Generate a machine-readable PRD payload with explicit task pass/fail criteria.
- Materialize PRD tasks into CHUM DAG tasks with deterministic IDs, labels, dependencies, and dedupe keys.
- Execute one PRD task per iteration, persist loop progress, and re-evaluate remaining tasks after each completion.
- Support `dry_run` mode that emits PRD + proposed tasks without mutating the DAG.
- Add tests covering report parsing, PRD generation schema validation, task materialization, and idempotent reruns.
design:
**Workflow**
- `CompoundProductWorkflow`
  1. `AnalyzeProductReportActivity`
  2. `GeneratePRDActivity`
  3. `MaterializePRDTasksActivity`
  4. `AdvancePRDIterationActivity`

**Storage**
- `compound_product_reports` (report hash, source, parsed findings JSON)
- `compound_prds` (prd id, version, status, schema payload)
- `compound_prd_tasks` (task key, pass criteria, linked CHUM task id, state)

**Execution Contract**
- Task schema includes `task_key`, `goal`, `verification`, `passes`.
- Only tasks with `passes=false` are filed or re-filed into CHUM.
- Loop writes compact progress snapshots for continuation and auditability.

**Integration**
- Expose CLI/API trigger for ad-hoc runs and optional cron hook for periodic report ingestion.

title: Promote validated crystallized skills into proteins and calcified scripts
description:
Turn successful repeated traces into safe deterministic genes that can execute
faster and cheaper than re-inference, with strict promotion and rollback gates.
acceptance:
- Define promotion thresholds (minimum support count, success rate, variance bounds) for crystal candidates.
- Candidates that pass thresholds are compiled into deterministic molecules/protein definitions or calcified scripts.
- New proteins run in shadow mode first, with side-by-side comparison against baseline execution.
- Automatic promotion to active only occurs after shadow validation passes configured gates.
- Automatic quarantine occurs on regression, with fallback to baseline path.
- Record token/time/quality deltas for each promoted candidate and include in learner telemetry.
design:
**Promotion Pipeline**
- `EvaluateCrystalCandidateActivity`
- `CompileCrystalToProteinActivity`
- `ShadowRunProteinActivity`
- `ActivateOrQuarantineProteinActivity`

**Store Integration**
- Reuse `proteins`, `protein_folds`, and `calcified_scripts` tables.
- Add lineage metadata linking `crystal_candidates.candidate_id` -> generated protein/script id.

**Execution Integration**
- Dispatcher queries active proteins for matching species and injects deterministic instructions early.
- If active crystal protein fails gating checks, quarantine and immediately revert to non-crystallized path.

**Governance**
- Promotion is feature-flagged and can be scoped by species/category.
- Keep explicit audit trail: who/what promoted, threshold values, and evaluation evidence.

title: Roll out compound memory/genetics stack with observability and kill switches
description:
Final slice: ship the stack safely with staged rollout, measurable outcomes,
and fast rollback paths so CHUM remains stable while compounding.
acceptance:
- Add independent feature flags for corpus ingestion, retrieval injection, product loop, and crystallization promotion.
- Emit metrics for memory retrieval hit quality, PRD loop throughput, crystal promotion rate, and regression incidents.
- Add health/status visibility for each subsystem in existing status surfaces.
- Provide a backfill command to import historical CHUM lessons/dispatch outcomes into the new memory corpus.
- Validate kill switches: each subsystem can be disabled at runtime without breaking core CHUM dispatch.
- Add an end-to-end smoke scenario that exercises the full td26 chain in fixture mode.
design:
**Flags**
- `[learner.compound].enabled`
- `[learner.compound_retrieval].enabled`
- `[groomer.compound_product].enabled`
- `[learner.crystallization].enabled`

**Metrics/Events**
- Retrieval: `compound_retrieval_hits`, `compound_retrieval_top_score`
- Product loop: `compound_prd_tasks_created`, `compound_prd_tasks_closed`
- Crystallization: `crystal_candidates_promoted`, `crystal_promotions_quarantined`

**Rollout Strategy**
- Stage 1: ingest-only (read path disabled)
- Stage 2: retrieval hints in prompts (non-blocking)
- Stage 3: product loop task filing enabled
- Stage 4: crystallization promotion enabled for selected species

**Operational Safety**
- Kill switch reverts to baseline behavior immediately.
- Document rollback and incident playbook for bad memory retrieval or bad crystal promotion.

title: Someday/Maybe: Auto Upgrade Seeker for external capability expansion
description: Design a new CHUM loop that continuously scouts external sources (GitHub, social channels, papers, release feeds) for high-signal upgrade opportunities and proposes capability-expanding changes. Goal is not just self-improvement of existing flows, but discovery and adoption of net-new capabilities.
acceptance:
- Define source allowlist and ingestion contract for external signals (APIs/RSS first, no unsafe scraping).
- Normalize candidate upgrades into a structured schema: problem, proposed technique, evidence links, maturity, license/security risk, expected impact, implementation cost.
- Implement ranking model for fit/impact/cost/risk and emit top-N upgrade briefs.
- Auto-file decomposed CHUM tasks only after threshold + policy checks pass.
- Add experiment lane with budget caps and promotion gates for adopted upgrades.
- Add observability for signal volume, conversion-to-shipped-capability rate, and false-positive rate.
design:
Proposed architecture: ScoutWorkflow -> NormalizeSignalsActivity -> ScoreUpgradeActivity -> GapMapActivity -> ProposeUpgradeBriefActivity -> Optional ExperimentWorkflow.

Key guardrails:
- License and security checks before filing implementation work
- Human gate before enabling production capability changes
- Kill switch for entire seeker subsystem

Rollout strategy:
1) Observe-only mode (no filing)
2) Suggest-only mode (briefs + candidate tasks)
3) Controlled experiment mode (sandbox execution)
4) Production capability promotion with explicit approval

title: Config-driven provider dispatch — eliminate hardcoded CLI harnesses
description:
## Problem

Provider/tier selection is config-driven in chum.toml (codex-first tiers with gemini fallback), but actual CLI invocation for shark workflows is hardcoded in agent_cli.go. Command templates are chosen by normalized agent name (codex, gemini, deepseek, claude) rather than using per-provider config.

Codex variants (codex-low/medium/high/extrahigh) are exposed in config but collapse to the same `codex exec ...` harness unless the model differs. The per-variant dispatch.cli args are not driving shark execution today. Model switches due to rate limits have been done ad-hoc, not via config.

## Current Execution Path

1. Provider selection comes from [tiers] + [providers], then CHUM builds escalation tiers with cli + model from provider config (workflow_dispatcher.go)
2. Each attempt sets req.Agent and req.Model from that tier (workflow.go)
3. CLI command templates are hardcoded:
   - codex: `codex exec --full-auto --json -m <model>`
   - gemini: `gemini -p "" --yolo -o json --model <model>`
   - deepseek: `deepseek --json --model <model>`
   - default: `claude --print --output-format json --dangerously-skip-permissions`
4. Review routing is cross-model from types.go: codex→gemini, gemini→codex, default codex
5. Backend routing in config is headless_cli for all tiers, but the main Temporal activity loop calls runAgent/runReviewAgent directly (not the generic backend abstraction)

## Required Changes

Each provider needs its own on/off switch and its own CLI config and model selection, driven entirely from config:

1. **Real harness control from config** — codex-low/medium/high/extrahigh, claude, gemini become truly distinct runtime paths (args/env/model)
2. **CLI command templates from [providers.*] config** — not hardcoded switch statements in agent_cli.go
3. **Per-provider enable/disable** — independent on/off switches rather than commenting out config blocks
4. **Deterministic fallback rules** — prevent accidental provider drift or surprise defaults
5. **Runtime budget gates** — hard per-provider caps and kill switches (not just config fields that are ignored)
6. **Startup preflight** — fail fast on missing CLI/auth/path before CHUM burns cycles on doomed dispatches
7. **Per-harness metrics/logs** — show which CLI/model failed, retried, escalated, and costed what
acceptance:
- Each provider in [providers.*] has an independent enabled flag that is respected at runtime
- CLI command construction reads args/env/model from provider config, not hardcoded templates
- codex-low vs codex-high produce measurably different runtime behavior (different args/timeouts)
- Provider switching (enable/disable, reorder tiers) works via config edit + restart only — no code changes
- Startup preflight validates CLI availability and auth for all enabled providers
- Per-provider token tracking and budget enforcement in the dispatcher
- Fallback chains are deterministic and logged
- No regression in existing shark execution or review routing
design:
### Phase 1: Provider Registry
Add a ProviderRegistry that loads from [providers.*] config and validates CLI/auth at startup. Each provider entry becomes a first-class runtime object with: cli_template, args, env, model, enabled, budget_cap, timeout.

### Phase 2: CLI Template Engine
Replace the hardcoded switch in agent_cli.go with a template-based command builder that reads from the registry. Support variable substitution for {model}, {prompt_file}, {workdir}.

### Phase 3: Runtime Controls
Per-provider enable/disable, per-provider token caps, startup preflight checks, and per-harness observability metrics.

### Phase 4: Migration
Gradual rollout — keep old hardcoded paths as fallback during transition, validate parity, then remove.

title: Per-organism dispatch pipeline with stigmergic artifact handoff
description: Refactor scheduler into organism-specific dispatch lanes: turtle-dispatcher ingests new/context-poor tasks for investigation/planning; crab-dispatcher consumes turtle-done artifacts for decomposition/sizing/dependency shaping; shark-dispatcher consumes ready morsels for execution. No direct handoff state machine required; artifacts in DAG/stores are the communication substrate.
acceptance: 1) Separate candidate selectors per organism with explicit eligibility rules. 2) New lifecycle labels/stages: intake -> turtle_done -> crab_done -> ready_for_shark. 3) Invariant checks prevent blocked tasks from entering ready_for_shark. 4) Dispatcher metrics by organism lane: candidate_count, dispatch_count, no_candidate_cycles, failure_rate. 5) Backward-compatible migration path from current single dispatcher.
design: Stigmergic pipeline: organisms publish durable artifacts (plans, decomp outputs, acceptance updates), and downstream lanes read those artifacts to decide eligibility. Avoid synchronous chain coupling.
notes: Filed from human-turtle planning session; motivated by crab inactivity and dispatcher scan failures.

title: Lean Minimal Mode fallback after planning failures
description: Add a Lean Minimal Mode so planning/decomposition never blocks dispatch. After 2 planning failures on the same task, auto-generate a parser-safe minimal template with exactly 3 deliverables (primary, validation, handoff), enforce a task sizing envelope (<=30m expected, <=3 files, single subsystem), and dispatch unblocked work.
acceptance: 1) Config flags: enable_consensus, enable_decomp, max_plan_retries, enable_minimal_fallback. 2) Fallback triggers after 2 planner failures for same task. 3) Generated plan includes '- [ ]' deliverables and passes parser. 4) Runtime scope-breach emits morsel and auto-splits. 5) Metrics exposed: parse_success_rate, child_start_rate, ready_but_blocked_rate, scope_breach_rate.
design: Execution-first profile with hard parser-safe schema and right-sizing gate. Planning is advisory; dispatch fails open to minimal mode for low-risk tasks.
notes: [remora] After chum-2c3d56 completed: Recent Temporal tests + agent_cli behavior now guarantee infrastructure-dead failures are classified in outcomes, short-circuit retries, and preserve circuit-breaker state; in chum-01f700, reuse this outcome signal when counting per-task planner failures for max_plan_retries and define whether infrastructure_dead should count toward the 2-failure Lean Minimal trigger.

title: Unify turtle_chat.go CLI harness with temporal agent_cli.go
description: matrix/turtle_chat.go:170 has its own runAgentCLI() with hardcoded switch statements for agent CLI commands. This is a copy of the logic in temporal/agent_cli.go — the two will drift as we make agent_cli config-driven. When the turtle system is reworked, this should be unified or at minimum made config-aware.
notes: [remora] After chum-2c3d56 completed: chum-2c3d56 added/validated new agent_cli semantics in internal/temporal (infrastructure-dead failover skip retries, preserve circuit-breaker state, record infrastructure_dead outcomes); matrix/turtle_chat.go runAgentCLI() remains a separate hardcoded switch and is likely missing future parity, so this unification item is now more urgent to prevent behavior mismatches.

title: Fix internal/temporal test suite panic: missing workflow registration in tests
description: Running `go test ./...` fails in `internal/temporal` due panic: `unable to find workflow type: AutonomousPlanningCeremonyWorkflow`. Test environments register only a subset of workflows, then child workflow execution path hits missing type and panics.
acceptance: 1) `go test ./internal/temporal/...` passes locally and in CI. 2) Tests that execute child workflows register all referenced workflow types (or use mocks that do not panic). 3) No panic from missing workflow type in Temporal test suite.
design: Audit temporal tests for workflow registration parity with production worker registration. Add helper to register complete workflow set in tests or explicitly stub child workflow invocations.
notes: Observed during CHUM workflow trace after ready-gate enforcement patch.

title: Species Catalog v0: candidate workflows to crystallize into proteins
description: Create a first-pass CHUM species catalog focused on repeatable, high-frequency work patterns that can be crystallized into deterministic proteins/scripts. Candidate species/workflows: go-bugfix, go-test-fix, go-lint-fix, temporal-workflow-patch, dispatcher-routing-fix, task-grooming-slice, provider-config-update, docs-runbook-update, security-remediation, release-hygiene.
acceptance: 1) Publish species list with naming convention, trigger heuristics, and expected inputs/outputs for each species. 2) For each species, define crystallization signal thresholds (success streak, confidence, guardrails). 3) Map each species to one initial protein scaffold and safe rollback path. 4) File follow-up morsels for top 3 highest-value species.
design: Leverage existing species classifier as baseline. Add explicit species taxonomy and protein candidacy matrix keyed by frequency, success rate, and blast radius. Keep v0 lightweight and operator-readable.

title: Codify overnight dispatcher/planning learnings into durable lessons
description: Capture last-night failure patterns and convert them into durable CHUM artifacts: (1) crab parse-plan requires at least one scope deliverable item; (2) turtle convergence failed because external binary claude was missing in PATH; (3) ready queue can contain stale items whose dependencies are no longer unblocked. Produce structured lessons plus follow-on guardrails.
acceptance: 1) Add at least 3 lessons tagged with categories antipattern/rule in lessons store (or equivalent CHUM ingestion path). 2) Each lesson links to a concrete failure signature and mitigation. 3) File or attach at least one guardrail task per systemic issue. 4) Include evidence query commands in task notes for future operators.
design: Implement as ops workflow: query health events plus workflow traces plus task dependency invariants; synthesize lessons; feed into learner-compatible artifact and/or direct store path. Treat this as reliability knowledge seeding, not one-off debugging.

title: Paleontologist: reconcile stale ready tasks against dependency DAG
description: Add a periodic reconciler (and startup pass) that demotes tasks from status=ready back to open when any dependency is unresolved. This closes the stale-ready loophole where a task can remain ready after dependency state changes.
acceptance: 1) Reconciler runs at least once per tick before candidate scan. 2) Any ready task with unresolved deps is demoted with a reason note/event. 3) Metrics include count of demoted stale-ready tasks. 4) Regression tests cover dependency changes after ready transition.
design: Use existing dependency query logic from ready-transition enforcement as shared predicate. Apply in scheduler/dispatcher pre-scan path and on startup inventory sweep. Emit health event type ready_reconciled for observability.

title: Paleontologist: emit workflow fitness metrics for learner and paleontologist
description: Expose first-class metrics and health events for ContinuousLearnerWorkflow and PaleontologistWorkflow runs (started/succeeded/failed, duration, items produced). Current metrics surface dispatches but not these adaptation loops.
acceptance: 1) Prometheus metrics include learner and paleontologist run counters by outcome. 2) Duration metrics available per workflow type. 3) Health events emitted on workflow failure with concise diagnosis. 4) Runbook/docs updated with query examples.
design: Instrument workflow entry/exit points and activity outcomes; persist summarized run result via store methods and expose via /metrics. Avoid high-cardinality labels.

title: Learner pilot species: temporal-test-fix protein scaffold
description: Define species taxonomy and initial deterministic scaffold for temporal test-suite repair tasks (missing workflow registration, harness mismatches, deterministic replay failures).
acceptance: 1) Species trigger heuristics documented. 2) One reusable protein scaffold script/prompt produced in shadow mode. 3) Safety guardrails defined (no auto-promote on flaky tests). 4) Backtest against at least 3 historical failures.
design: Seed from recent issue chum-960898 and related temporal test incidents; use shadow scoring before active promotion.

title: Learner pilot species: dispatch-preflight-fix protein scaffold
description: Crystallize the repeated dispatch infrastructure failure mode (missing binaries, PATH/env drift, gateway connectivity) into a deterministic preflight species scaffold.
acceptance: 1) Species trigger heuristics documented for infra-dead failures. 2) Deterministic preflight scaffold defined with hard fail-fast checks. 3) Antibody signatures listed for common failure strings. 4) Links to existing related morsels (e.g. preflight validation/escalated env failures).
design: Use exit-code 127, missing binary, gateway unavailable and malformed CLI invocation traces as classifier seeds; scaffold outputs actionable remediation before shark dispatch.

title: Learner pilot species: planning-output-sanitization protein scaffold
description: Crystallize planner outputs into a strict minimal schema so crab decomposition always gets at least one valid scope deliverable and explicit dependencies.
acceptance: 1) Minimal planning schema specified and validated before decomposition. 2) Invalid plans are repaired or rejected with explicit feedback. 3) Historical parse failures mapped to antibodies. 4) Include fallback behavior for low-context minimal mode.
design: Implement a sanitization layer between turtle output and crab parser; ensure deterministic formatting constraints and auto-repair rules for missing scope lines.

title: Fix pre-commit lint hook to scope to changed packages only
description: The golangci-lint pre-commit hook lints the entire repo, not just staged files. Pre-existing lint failures in store_test.go (govet shadow warnings) block unrelated commits. Change to `golangci-lint run --new-from-rev=HEAD` or scope to changed packages.

title: Fix store_test.go govet shadow warnings blocking lint
description: store_test.go has 5 govet shadow warnings (lines 2049, 2054, 2068, 2111, 2115) that block pre-commit hooks for all packages. These are pre-existing and have blocked at least 3 sessions. Fix by renaming inner err variables.

title: DoD-Local Scope Gate for Sub-Step Failure Handling
description: Scope contracts and invariants for sub-step failure handling remain local to the active DoD check and must not affect review/UBS/execute sequencing.
acceptance: A typed DoD-sub-step failure contract exists in temporal workflow types and is consumed in workflow state transitions with documented invariants; prune/escalate decisions are only applied at DoD check boundaries; existing non-DoD pipelines remain unchanged.

title: Structured DoD Check Failure Fingerprinting
description: Introduce typed, normalized failure signatures for DoD check failures and flow them end-to-end through check reporting.
acceptance: DoD verification emits machine-readable failure signature data (command token, exit bucket/class, normalized output features, bounded snippets), and no behavior regressions are introduced in existing string-only display paths.

title: Per-Morsel/Sub-Step Prune Counters and Tier Escalation
description: Track per-morsel/sub-step failure history in-memory and enforce local retry/prune/escalation decisions without global side effects.
acceptance: Workflow records prune count, latest signature, and active local tier per morsel+sub-step; repeated sub-step failures map to deterministic local control behavior.

title: Persist Repeated Sub-Step Failures as Genome Antibodies + Hibernation Trigger
description: On thresholded repeated sub-step failures, persist structured evidence and route through existing genome learning path with hibernation/safety behavior.
acceptance: Third repeat on a morsel+sub-step consistently persists typed failure metadata and can trigger existing hibernation/blocking controls without altering non-DoD behavior.

title: FailureTriage Feedback Wiring and Production Verification Hooks
description: Capture triage guidance for sub-steps and expose non-invasive counters/telemetry around matching, escalation, and hibernation behavior.
acceptance: Triage output includes optional sub-step recommendations and similarity metadata; workflow consumes these hints in local decisioning and reports counters for match and escalation events.

title: Whale 0 / Morsel 0: Define DoD sub-step scope contract in workflow/types
description: Add explicit types/constants/state schema for DoD-local sub-step failure tracking and document invariants: prune/escalate applies only at DoD check boundaries and does not alter review/UBS/execute sequence.
acceptance: Shared temporal types include an additive DoD sub-step contract (sub-step signature, local state, constants for boundaries); invariants are codified in comments/validation; no existing workflow branching outside DoD check handlers is modified.
design: Introduce additive types in `internal/temporal/types.go` for sub-step signature/state (e.g., boundary enum, prune/escalate intent, immutable transition marker) and constants describing strict boundary invariants. Keep compatibility by only extending existing structs and guarding default values to preserve old JSON decode behavior. In `internal/temporal/workflow.go`, enforce all prune/escalate decisions only inside DoD check decision points so review/UBS/execute ordering remains unchanged. Avoid any token parsing/composition changes in this workstream to respect the existing plan-token accounting rule.

title: Whale 1 / Morsel 0: Add normalized failure signature model and normalizer
description: Introduce CheckFailureSignature (or equivalent) with command token, exit-code class/bucket, and normalized stderr/stdout feature extraction plus truncation and hashing controls.
acceptance: A deterministic normalizer API exists that maps command output/exit code into stable buckets and fixed-length signature inputs; class mapping is strict and documented; truncation/hash behavior is bounded and reproducible.
design: Implement additive failure signature primitives in `internal/temporal/types.go` and normalization helpers in `internal/git/merge.go` with side-effect-free pure functions. Define strict exit-code classes/buckets (e.g., success, soft-fail, infra-fail, hard-fail, timeout, unknown) and deterministic token extraction order to avoid replay drift. Ensure output snippets are normalized (e.g., whitespace/path/cwd normalization as needed) then truncated to fixed maxima before hashing so signatures are stable and bounded. Keep behavior conservative so existing call paths receive no semantic changes unless they opt into typed signatures.

title: Whale 1 / Morsel 1: Emit structured check failures from DoDVerifyActivity
description: Update DoDVerifyActivity and RecordOutcome payload path to pass typed check_failures (signature, command, duration_ms, exit code, bounded snippets) instead of only flattened string messages.
acceptance: Typed failure arrays are emitted and persisted through `RecordOutcome`; existing `message` string field remains populated for backward compatibility; all existing payload consumers continue to decode.
design: Extend activity output/input payload structs in `internal/temporal/types.go` with typed `check_failures` while leaving existing flattened message fields intact and additive-only. In `internal/temporal/activities.go`, map DoD command results into both typed and legacy fields, preserving full duration/exit/stderr/stdout bounded extraction. Ensure any token- or message-related derived fields do not trigger additional untracked token composition logic; existing `totalTokens` composition pathways stay unchanged.

title: Whale 2 / Morsel 0: Add in-memory per-morsel/sub-step state in DoD workflow loop
description: Introduce map-backed state (map[morselID]map[substepSig]*SubstepState) in workflow execution context storing prune count, last signature, and local tier index.
acceptance: Workflow state context contains isolated mutable-safe per-morsel/sub-step state with prune counters, last signature, and per-sub-step local tier index; no cross-stage reference leaks across replay cycles.
design: Add state types and fields in `internal/temporal/types.go` and instantiate/use maps in `internal/temporal/workflow.go` inside DoD loop handling. Keep updates monotonic (increment counters; assign tier transitions once per failed attempt). Store copies/immutable snapshots for replay safety rather than sharing references among stages. Ensure map initialization is deterministic in workflow replay context and does not alter unrelated sequence control.

title: Whale 2 / Morsel 1: Add local prune and skip behavior for repeated matching failures
description: Modify DoD execution to detect repeated matching sub-step failures and prune at the matching command boundary, skipping unrelated stages and continuing with local policy.
acceptance: Repeated matching failures on the same sub-step trigger local prune once threshold is crossed; skipping is scoped only to matching command boundary and does not alter non-DoD sequence ordering.
design: In `internal/temporal/workflow.go`, compare new normalized signatures from typed failures against stored per-sub-step signature state and maintain prune counters. Implement a local decision gate that only prunes when same sub-step signature repeats and threshold is hit, skipping unrelated DoD stage entries while preserving contract boundaries. Preserve existing global retry and escalation semantics; this is strictly local-policy in DoD flow. Keep counters/metrics side-effect free from control path decisions.

title: Whale 2 / Morsel 2: Advance per-sub-step tier using TIER_CHAIN during local retries
description: Use local sub-step state to choose the next DoD check tier from TIER_CHAIN after repeated failure conditions are met.
acceptance: Per-sub-step state drives deterministic local tier selection from existing `TIER_CHAIN` for repeated failures without affecting other morsels' or global morsel-tier state.
design: Extend the `internal/temporal/workflow.go` DoD retry loop to consult per-(morsel, sub-step) state and advance local tier through `TIER_CHAIN` only for repeated failure thresholds. Isolate selection logic to the sub-step local context and avoid global side effects. Keep behavior deterministic/replay-safe using existing state containers from prior morsel and deterministic chain traversal.

title: Whale 3 / Morsel 0: Build typed genome failure entry from sub-step metadata
description: Extend failure entry payload so RecordFailureActivity/EvolveGenomeWithCost can persist command/signature/classification/files/context for a sub-step failure.
acceptance: Failure entry schema includes typed sub-step metadata fields with defaults for unknown/new readers; storage/readers remain backward-compatible; existing entries remain valid.
design: Add optional typed failure-entry fields in `internal/temporal/types.go` and thread through `internal/temporal/activities.go` into store-facing structure in `internal/store/genomes.go`. Keep fields additive/nullable and provide fallback defaults when consumers do not provide metadata. Do not alter existing mutation costs or success conditions unless typed metadata is available. No token parsing shortcuts that bypass `totalTokens` flows.

title: Whale 3 / Morsel 1: Invoke repeated-prune antibody learning path
description: On the third repeated prune for a morsel+sub-step, route through existing RecordFailure -> EvolveGenomeWithCost path with doDPassed=false and typed entry.
acceptance: Third repeated local prune triggers existing mutation path once, with `doDPassed=false`, typed failure entry included, and no new control-plane mutation path introduced.
design: Add a single branch in `internal/temporal/workflow.go` DoD retry loop that checks local prune count and on threshold invokes existing `RecordFailureActivity` flow, passing typed entry produced earlier. In `internal/temporal/activities.go`, map this path to `EvolveGenomeWithCost` unchanged aside from additional metadata fields. Preserve current safety gates and avoid rewriting existing mutation hooks.

title: Whale 3 / Morsel 2: Trigger hibernation after third prune using existing safety controls
description: Use existing stage cooldown/attempt window and safety_blocks config/flow to hibernate or block repeating bad routes after third prune.
acceptance: After third local prune, existing safety/cooldown controls are engaged via current config gates; no new safety policy system is introduced.
design: Wire the third-prune branch in `internal/temporal/workflow.go` to existing cooldown and safety-block configuration (`internal/config`) paths used by other repeated-failure outcomes. Ensure this decision is deterministic and only reuses established blocking semantics. Keep the feature additive and config-driven; avoid inventing new failure codes or hard-coded windows.

title: Whale 4 / Morsel 0: Extend FailureTriageActivity contract for sub-step signals
description: Add minimal fields for triage output: recommended sub-step key, similarity score/reason, and optional escalate/skip suggestions while preserving existing triage semantics.
acceptance: Triage response includes optional sub-step signal fields without breaking legacy consumers; when absent, behavior remains identical to current triage output.
design: Add optional fields to triage payload structs in `internal/temporal/types.go` and return them from `internal/temporal/activities.go` with zero behavior change when model/path does not populate them. Keep additions additive and ensure existing decision defaults remain unchanged.

title: Whale 4 / Morsel 1: Consume triage signals and add non-invasive observability
description: Apply triage hints to local sub-step decisioning and emit counters for signature match, prune count per step, sub-step escalations, and hibernation trigger.
acceptance: Workflow can consume optional triage hints to influence local decisions when present, while maintaining current behavior when absent; low-cardinality counters are emitted without blocking the critical path.
design: In `internal/temporal/workflow.go`, read optional triage fields and apply only additive influence to local sub-step policies (e.g., prefer escalate/skip hints). Add telemetry counters for signature matches, prune counts by sub-step, escalations, and hibernation triggers through existing non-blocking metrics infrastructure, consistent with observability as platform infrastructure (per-worker principle) and avoid heavy-cardinality labels. Ensure metrics failures do not fail workflow execution.

title: Add FitnessScore and ProbeOnly fields to EscalationTier

acceptance: EscalationTier struct has FitnessScore float64 and ProbeOnly bool fields with correct json tags. go build ./... passes. go vet ./... passes. Existing tests remain green (zero-value defaults preserve behavior).
design: Add fields after the existing Enabled field at types.go:38. Use json tags 'fitness_score' and 'probe_only' matching the snake_case convention of existing fields (provider_key, etc.). FitnessScore float64 `json:"fitness_score"` and ProbeOnly bool `json:"probe_only"`. Go zero-values (0.0, false) mean no existing struct literal needs updating — all current EscalationTier constructions in workflow_dispatcher.go:968 and workflow.go:383-389 will compile without changes. Historical context warns about checking existing type definitions first — confirmed EscalationTier is only in types.go:32-39.

title: Add Store.AggregateProviderFitness for cold-start fallback
description: Add a new method AggregateProviderFitness(project string) (map[string]float64, error) to the Store. This queries all genomes, aggregates ProviderGene success/failure/cost across all species, and returns a map of provider→fitness score. Used as cold-start fallback when no species-specific genome exists.
acceptance: Store.AggregateProviderFitness method exists and compiles. It queries all genomes, aggregates ProviderGene data across species, computes fitness using same formula as ProviderGene.Fitness(). Returns empty map (not nil) when no genomes exist. Unit tests cover: empty DB, single genome with multiple providers, multiple genomes with overlapping providers, zero-cost edge case. go build/vet/test pass.
design: Add method to internal/store/genomes.go after SpreadPhages (line 410). Query: SELECT provider_genes FROM genomes WHERE provider_genes != '[]'. For each row, unmarshal []ProviderGene JSON, accumulate successes/failures/cost per provider key into a temporary map[string]*ProviderGene. After accumulation, compute Fitness() for each aggregated gene and return map[string]float64. The project parameter is accepted but currently unused (future: filter by project-associated species). Use the same Fitness() formula from ProviderGene.Fitness() (line 34-45): successRate / costPerAttempt, with the free=×1000 heuristic. Create genomes_test.go with TestAggregateProviderFitness using in-memory SQLite (same pattern as existing store tests). Historical context: 'Provider genes are selfish genes — they track per-model fitness' — this aggregation is the population-level view of those selfish genes.

title: Expand buildEscalationTiers signature to accept species
description: Change buildEscalationTiers to accept an optional species string parameter. Pass it from the call site in ScanCandidatesActivity (workflow_dispatcher.go:578). For now the species parameter is accepted but unused — the function body does not change. This is a pure signature refactor to prepare for fitness-aware routing.
acceptance: buildEscalationTiers accepts species string as 4th parameter. Call site at line 578 passes empty string. Function body unchanged. go build/vet pass. No test changes needed (function is not directly tested yet).
design: Change signature at workflow_dispatcher.go:939 from buildEscalationTiers(cfg *config.Config, st *store.Store, logger interface{ Warn(string, ...any) }) to buildEscalationTiers(cfg *config.Config, st *store.Store, logger interface{ Warn(string, ...any) }, species string). Update call site at line 578: buildEscalationTiers(cfg, da.Store, logger, ""). The species parameter is unused in the function body — add a blank identifier if go vet complains, but since it's a string parameter that will be used in M4, just leave it named. Historical context: 'The dispatcher is the shark feeder' — species awareness lets the feeder optimize which sharks get which food.

title: Wire fitness-based tier reordering into buildEscalationTiers
description: Modify buildEscalationTiers to use genome data for routing. After building the config-based chain, if a species genome exists with ProviderGenes, reorder enabled tiers by ProviderGene.Fitness() descending. If no species genome exists, fall back to AggregateProviderFitness (cold-start). Implement graduated throttling: fitness < 0.20 → ProbeOnly=true, fitness < 0.05 → Enabled=false.
acceptance: buildEscalationTiers reorders tiers by fitness when genome data exists. ProbeOnly=true for fitness < 0.20. Enabled=false for fitness < 0.05. Cold-start falls back to AggregateProviderFitness. When no genome data exists at all, config ordering is preserved. FitnessScore field is populated on each tier. go build/vet pass.
design: After the existing tier-building loop (workflow_dispatcher.go:940-977), add a fitness-aware reordering block. Steps: (1) If species is empty, return tiers as-is (preserve existing behavior for non-species-aware callers). (2) Call st.GetGenome(species). If genome has ProviderGenes, build providerKey→fitness map from genome.ProviderGenes[i].Fitness(). (3) If genome is empty (Generation==0), call st.AggregateProviderFitness("") for cold-start fallback. (4) If neither source has data, return tiers unchanged. (5) For each tier, look up fitness score by tier.ProviderKey. Set tier.FitnessScore. Apply thresholds: <0.05 → Enabled=false, <0.20 → ProbeOnly=true. (6) Sort tiers by FitnessScore descending using sort.SliceStable (stable preserves config order for equal-fitness tiers). Guard all Store calls with nil checks (st != nil). Log reordering decisions at Info level. Historical context: 'Provider genes are selfish genes' — this is where the selfish gene actually influences organism behavior.

title: Honor ProbeOnly in workflow execution and add probe counter
description: In workflow.go's ChumAgentWorkflow, modify the chain iteration (line 396-400) to handle ProbeOnly tiers: instead of skipping disabled tiers entirely, implement a 1-in-10 probe mechanism for ProbeOnly tiers using workflow.Now(ctx).UnixNano()%10==0. Add a log line for probe activations and skips.
acceptance: ProbeOnly tiers are executed ~10% of the time based on workflow.Now(ctx).UnixNano()%10==0. ProbeOnly tiers that don't fire are skipped with an info log. ProbeOnly tiers that fire are executed normally with an info log. Non-ProbeOnly tiers are completely unaffected. go build/vet pass.
design: In workflow.go at the tier loop (line 396), add a check BEFORE the existing !tier.Enabled check. Logic: if tier.ProbeOnly { ts := workflow.Now(ctx).UnixNano(); if ts%10 != 0 { logger.Info(SharkPrefix+' Skipping probe-only provider', 'Provider', tier.ProviderKey, 'Tier', tier.Tier, 'Timestamp', ts); continue }; logger.Info(SharkPrefix+' PROBE: activating probe-only provider', 'Provider', tier.ProviderKey, 'Tier', tier.Tier) }. This must come before the !tier.Enabled check because ProbeOnly tiers might also have Enabled=false (for fitness < 0.05). The probe mechanism uses Temporal's deterministic workflow.Now() — NOT time.Now() — which is critical for replay safety. The %10==0 gives ~10% activation rate. Historical context: 'Every organism is mortal' — probes are expendable organisms that test whether a degraded provider has recovered.

title: Add unit tests for fitness-aware tier reordering in buildEscalationTiers
description: Write unit tests for buildEscalationTiers with genome data: verify fitness reordering, graduated throttling at 0.20 and 0.05 thresholds, cold-start fallback to config ordering when no genome exists, and cold-start fallback to AggregateProviderFitness when species genome is empty but global data exists.
acceptance: Tests cover: (1) fitness reordering with species genome, (2) ProbeOnly at fitness < 0.20, (3) Enabled=false at fitness < 0.05, (4) cold-start with empty species genome falls back to AggregateProviderFitness, (5) no genome data preserves config ordering, (6) empty species string preserves config ordering. All tests pass. go test ./internal/temporal/... passes.
design: Create internal/temporal/workflow_dispatcher_test.go. Use in-memory SQLite for Store (store.New(':memory:')). Build config.Config with 3 providers (codex-spark, claude-rescue, gemini-flash) across tiers. For fitness reordering test: seed genome with ProviderGenes where claude-rescue has highest fitness, verify tiers are reordered. For throttling tests: seed genome with provider fitness values at 0.19, 0.04, verify ProbeOnly and Enabled flags. For cold-start test: don't seed species genome but seed other genomes with provider data, verify AggregateProviderFitness is used. For no-data test: empty DB, verify config order preserved. Need to create a minimal logger mock that satisfies interface{ Warn(string, ...any) }. Historical context: 'agents that ran tests had a 0% failure rate' — these tests are the safety net for the core routing change.

title: Add unit tests for ProbeOnly tier handling in ChumAgentWorkflow
description: Write unit tests for ProbeOnly tier handling in ChumAgentWorkflow: verify that ProbeOnly tiers are skipped ~90% of the time and executed ~10% of the time based on workflow timestamp. Verify that probe activations are logged. Verify that non-ProbeOnly tiers are unaffected.
acceptance: Tests verify: (1) ProbeOnly tier is skipped when workflow.Now().UnixNano()%10 != 0, (2) ProbeOnly tier is executed when workflow.Now().UnixNano()%10 == 0, (3) non-ProbeOnly tiers execute normally regardless of timestamp. All tests pass.
design: Add tests to internal/temporal/workflow_test.go. Use Temporal's testsuite.TestWorkflowEnvironment. The key challenge is controlling workflow.Now() — Temporal test environments don't directly expose SetStartTime, but you can use env.SetStartTime() (available in newer SDK versions) or create a wrapper workflow that uses a known timestamp. Alternative: test with two specific timestamps — one where UnixNano()%10==0 and one where it doesn't — and verify the execution/skip behavior. Construct TaskRequest with EscalationChain containing a ProbeOnly tier. For the 'probe activates' test: pick a start time where UnixNano()%10==0, verify ExecuteActivity is called for that tier. For the 'probe skipped' test: pick a time where %10!=0, verify the tier is skipped (execute not called). Use the existing stubActivities pattern from workflow_test.go. Note: workflow.go line 392-394 truncates chain to 1 tier in fast-loop mode — tests may need to account for this or test with a single-tier chain.

Add EarlyKill EarlyKillConfig field to the Dispatch struct. Wire defaults in applyDefaults() in defaults.go. Add [dispatch.early_kill] section to chum.toml.

Use `toml:"early_kill"` struct tag on the Dispatch field. In defaults.go, add defaults inside the existing applyDefaults function following the same nil/zero-check pattern used for RetryPolicy. For chum.toml, add the section under [dispatch]. Use identical declaration style: `var ErrInfrastructureDead = fmt.Errorf("infrastructure dead: agent produced insufficient output")`. Keep the error message descriptive for log grep-ability. This is a one-line addition with zero risk.

title: Implement early-kill check in runCLI heartbeat loop
description: In runCLI (agent_cli.go:335), add startTime := time.Now() before the heartbeat loop. In the heartbeat case, read config thresholds and check output file size at 3m and 8m elapsed. Kill process and return ErrInfrastructureDead if below threshold.
acceptance: After 3 minutes, process is killed if output file < MinBytes3m. After 8 minutes, killed if < MinBytes8m. Check is skipped when EarlyKill.Enabled is false. Process kill is clean (SIGKILL or cmd.Process.Kill). ErrInfrastructureDead is returned. `go build ./...` passes.
design: Add `startTime := time.Now()` before the heartbeat select loop. Inside the heartbeat ticker case, after RecordHeartbeat, read config via `a.ConfigManager.Get().Dispatch.EarlyKill`. Use `os.Stat(outputFile)` to get file size — do NOT read the file. Guard with `if !cfg.Enabled { continue }`. Check `elapsed := time.Since(startTime)`: if elapsed > 3m and size < MinBytes3m, or elapsed > 8m and size < MinBytes8m, call `cmd.Process.Kill()`, set a flag, and break. After the loop, if the flag is set, return ErrInfrastructureDead. Historical context: every CLI output flag change must update parsers — but this morsel doesn't change output flags, just monitors file size. Be careful not to race with the process exit path — check cmd.ProcessState before killing.
notes: [remora] After chum-2a757e completed: Dependency/context update: runAgentWithFailover already classifies ErrInfrastructureDead as infrastructure-terminal (no circuit-breaker accounting, no next-agent failover). This backlog item remains required: implement 3m/8m output-size checks in runCLI heartbeat loop and return ErrInfrastructureDead after killing the process when below thresholds.

title: Add unit tests for early-kill behavior
description: Add tests covering: process killed when output < threshold after 3m, process NOT killed when output is sufficient, early_kill disabled skips check.
acceptance: Three test cases pass: (1) low-output process killed with ErrInfrastructureDead, (2) sufficient-output process completes normally, (3) disabled config means no kill. Tests use short ticker intervals. `go test ./internal/temporal/... -run EarlyKill` passes.
design: Use table-driven tests following existing patterns in agent_cli_test.go. Use mockConfigManager to control EarlyKill settings. Create a mock process (e.g., `sleep` or a small Go binary) that writes a controlled amount to a temp file. Override the heartbeat ticker interval to something short (100ms) and set MinBytes3m/MinBytes8m thresholds low for test speed. Set startTime artificially or use a short duration. Historical context: CLI agents with JSON output mode wrap content differently — but these tests should use raw file size checks, not parsing.
notes: [remora] After chum-9b2a9d completed: Implementation is in place in runCLI plus config/default/validation wiring; remaining scope is explicit unit coverage for the 3 acceptance cases: (1) low-output process is killed and returns ErrInfrastructureDead after threshold time, (2) sufficient-output process is not killed and exits normally, (3) early_kill disabled skips watchdog kill checks; use short ticker/watchdog intervals and deterministic test fixtures.

title: Handle ErrInfrastructureDead in runAgentWithFailover
description: In runAgentWithFailover, add errors.Is check for ErrInfrastructureDead. Skip circuit breaker recording and break retry loop immediately.
acceptance: When runCLI returns ErrInfrastructureDead: (1) errKind is set to 'infrastructure_dead', (2) agentFailureTracker is NOT called, (3) retry loop exits immediately without trying next agent. `go build ./...` passes.
design: In the failure handling block around line 472 of agent_cli.go, add `if errors.Is(err, ErrInfrastructureDead)` BEFORE the circuit breaker recording logic. Set `errKind = "infrastructure_dead"` (matching the constant from morsel 5). Use a labeled break (`break agentLoop`) or set a flag and break. Do NOT record failure in agentFailureTracker — infrastructure death is not the agent's fault. Historical context: scope drift is a known risk in this file — touch ONLY the failover function, do not modify parsers or other functions.
notes: [remora] After chum-ae9843 completed: ErrInfrastructureDead now exists in internal/temporal/agent_cli.go; runAgentWithFailover still only special-cases ErrModelExhausted. Add an errors.Is(err, ErrInfrastructureDead) branch that sets errKind='infrastructure_dead', does not call agentFailureTracker/circuit-breaker recording, and exits the retry loop immediately without trying the next agent, then verify with `go build ./...`.

title: Add infrastructure_dead category to failure classifier

design: Add `if errors.Is(err, ErrInfrastructureDead) { result.FailureCategory = FailureCategoryInfrastructureDead }` at each call site after runAgentWithFailover returns. If 3+ call sites share identical logic, consider a small helper, but do NOT over-abstract — the check is a single if-statement. Import the sentinel error if needed. In `activities.go`, `crab_activities.go`, `turtle_activities.go`, `learner_activities.go`, and `planning_activities.go`, add `errors.Is(err, ErrInfrastructureDead)` handling to set `result.FailureCategory = "infrastructure_dead"` wherever runAgentWithFailover errors are converted into dispatch outcomes.

acceptance: Tests pass for: (1) only first agent called when ErrInfrastructureDead returned, (2) circuit breaker state unchanged after infrastructure_dead, (3) activity outcome has failure_category=infrastructure_dead. `go test ./internal/temporal/...` passes.

For activity tests: use existing test patterns in activities_test.go, mock runAgentWithFailover to return ErrInfrastructureDead, assert result.FailureCategory. Historical context: follow table-driven test patterns. Do not delete or modify existing tests.

Keep this item open and narrow remaining scope to AC(2) verify circuit-breaker state is unchanged on `infrastructure_dead`, and AC(3) verify activity outcomes set `failure_category` to `infrastructure...`.
